categories.name: US Birds
categories.note: North American bird species — common backyard birds, raptors, waterfowl, songbirds. Names solvers might encounter on a hike or at a feeder. Avoid obscure subspecies.

categories.name: Plants
categories.note: Trees, flowers, garden plants, crops, common herbs. Recognizable species, not Latin taxonomy.

categories.name: Animals
categories.note: Mammals, reptiles, amphibians, fish, insects — anything that isn't a bird (separate category) or plant. Common species and notable wildlife.

categories.name: Other nature
categories.note: Weather (clouds, rain types, seasons, climate zones), ecosystems and biomes (tundra, savanna, coral reef), Earth processes (tides, erosion, photosynthesis), natural materials (amber, granite, obsidian). Earth-relevant astronomy (moon phases, eclipses, equinox) ONLY. Excludes named storms/volcanoes/earthquakes (→ Science), named geographic features like rivers/mountains (→ Geography), and specific animals/plants/birds.

categories.name: Anime/manga
categories.note: Anime and manga titles, characters, studios (Ghibli, Toei), creators (Miyazaki, Toriyama). Spans classic (Astro Boy, DBZ) through current (Chainsaw Man, JJK, Frieren). Live-action adaptations of anime IP still count here.

categories.name: Movies
categories.note: Live-action and animated films — titles, characters, directors, iconic lines, franchises. Exclude anime films (→ Anime/manga) and superhero-comics-origin films when the clue references the comics canon (→ Comics).

categories.name: TV
categories.note: Live-action and Western-animated television — sitcoms, dramas, reality, animated series (Simpsons, Family Guy, Bluey). Streaming counts. Exclude anime (→ Anime/manga).

categories.name: Books
categories.note: Novels, authors, characters, literary movements. Both classic literature and contemporary bestsellers. Manga goes in Anime/manga; comics in Comics.

categories.name: Videogames
categories.note: Game titles, characters, studios, in-game terminology. Spans retro (Pac-Man, Zelda) through current (Elden Ring, Stardew Valley). Consoles and gaming HARDWARE go in Tech.

categories.name: Music
categories.note: Songs, albums, lyrics, bands, artists-as-musicians, instruments, genres, music theory terms. Artist personal-life trivia goes in Celebrities/athletes.

categories.name: YouTubers/internet
categories.note: YouTubers, streamers, TikTokers, memes, viral moments, internet slang, defunct sites (Vine, Tumblr), online subcultures. Internet-native culture.

categories.name: Celebrities/athletes
categories.note: Famous PEOPLE — actors, musicians, athletes, public figures, by name. Their biographies, personal lives, signature looks. Their WORK goes in the relevant medium.

categories.name: Comics/superheroes
categories.note: Marvel, DC, Image, indie comics — characters, runs, creators, story arcs. Clues that reference the comics canon. Movie/TV adaptations go in Movies/TV unless the clue is comics-specific.

categories.name: Art
categories.note: Visual art — paintings, sculpture, painters, art movements (Impressionism, Cubism), museums, art history. Includes fine-art photography.

categories.name: History
categories.note: Real, documented events and people — wars, treaties, monarchs, civil rights, exploration, named historical figures. Architecture-as-period (Gothic, Baroque) lives here.

categories.name: Mythology/folklore/religion
categories.note: Stories, deities, scripture, fairy tales, urban legends, religious figures, holidays-as-tradition. Spans ancient myth (Zeus, Odin, Ra) and active religion (apostles, surahs, the Eightfold Path, saints). Real historical religious figures (Martin Luther) go in History; figures who exist only in scripture/myth (Moses) go here.

categories.name: Science/space
categories.note: Physics, biology, chemistry, geology, astronomy, space exploration, scientists, elements, classical inventions (steam engine, telegraph), named natural disasters (Vesuvius, Krakatoa). Computing/internet/gadgets go in Tech.

categories.name: Tech/computing
categories.note: Computing, internet, programming languages, gadgets, tech companies, software, AI, gaming hardware/consoles. Post-1950 tech that exists because of computers.

categories.name: Sports
categories.note: Events, teams, leagues, terminology, iconic moments, rules. NOT the athletes themselves (→ Celebrities/athletes). 'Super Bowl XLVIII' yes; 'Tom Brady' no.

categories.name: Food & drink
categories.note: Dishes, ingredients, cuisines, beverages, cooking terms, chefs. Regional dishes count here; regions known for food (Emilia-Romagna) go in Geography.

categories.name: Dictionary
categories.note: Words clued by their definition, etymology, or synonyms — clues are about the word itself, not what it refers to. Lean toward common-to-medium difficulty. AVOID obscure/archaic words and rare synonyms — the v1 puzzle was criticized for too many hard dictionary clues.

categories.name: Geography
categories.note: Countries, cities, rivers, mountains, deserts, oceans BY NAME. Landmarks (Eiffel Tower, Great Wall). Capitals, regions, geographic features.

categories.name: Hand curated
categories.note: Hand-authored clues (image/audio/video plus text-only pop-culture/pen trivia — not all include media) — authored separately in media-clues.json and merged at layout time. The generation script SKIPS this category. 49 entries authored; the former 51-clue surplus was redistributed across the generated categories.

v1Feedback.tooMuchDictionary: v1 had too many dictionary clues, and too many of them were difficult. Cut the count significantly and target common-to-medium difficulty.
v1Feedback.tooSimilar: v1 clues felt too similar to each other in style and difficulty. Vary tone, structure, and difficulty across the set.
v1Feedback.skewedHard: v1 skewed too hard, making solving feel like a grind. Aim for a wider difficulty range — easy/medium clues should outnumber hard ones roughly 2:1. Do NOT overcorrect to uniformly easy.
v1Feedback.moreUSBirds: Players asked for more US bird clues specifically.
v1Feedback.moreAnime: Players asked for substantially more anime/manga clues.
v1Feedback.morePopCulture: Players asked for substantially more pop culture overall.

styles.crossword: Traditional concise crossword clue: short, often a definition or wordplay. Example clue for JUPITER: 'Largest planet in our solar system'.
styles.jeopardy: Declarative statement framed like a Jeopardy answer — full sentence, often factual narration. The solver still types the word; only the clue text style differs. Example clue for JUPITER: 'This gas giant has a Great Red Spot that has been raging for at least 350 years'.
